name: Secure Boot
def: A boot process that cryptographically verifies each stage before executing it, establishing a chain of trust from immutable hardware up to the OS.
problem: Malware that subverts firmware/bootloader (bootkits, rootkits) loads before the OS and any defences, making it nearly invisible.
mechanism: A hardware root of trust verifies the firmware’s signature, which verifies the bootloader, which verifies the kernel — each link checked before control passes. Measured boot additionally records hashes in a TPM for later attestation.
benefits: Enables remote attestation of platform state
limits: Can hinder legitimate custom OS / dual-boot
Trust depends on the vendor key hierarchy
failures: Leaked / mis-scoped signing keys undermining the chain
Trusting firmware that itself is vulnerable
whenNot: Threat model excludes physical / boot-level attackers and the friction is unjustified
myths: "Secure boot stops all malware" — it protects the boot chain, not runtime exploitation
